context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on the city stressors table tbh. can u just explain the whole assignment from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get unstuck! Before we dive in - have you already chosen your case study city? And have you looked at the stressors table template at all yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student says they're lost but hasn't told me anything about their work so far. Before giving any explanation, I should find out where they actually are - what city they've chosen, what they've done so far. This avoids over-scaffolding and lets me target my support. I'll ask a focused question to orient the conversation.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but rn i'm confused about basically all of it ngl. i looked at the template and it still didn't click.
exchanges.tutor: Ok, let's start simple. What city did you pick? Once we have that, tell me: what's one climate-related problem you already know that city faces?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being vague about what they don't understand. Rather than explaining the whole assignment (which would violate the guided discovery rule), I should get them to commit to a city first, and then narrow down what specifically is confusing. Starting with the most concrete first step will help them gain traction.

exchanges.student: i picked jakarta i think, but now i'm not even sure what counts as a “stressor” vs just like a normal city problem fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good question - that distinction matters. So think about Jakarta: you probably know it has serious flooding issues. Do you think that flooding is connected to climate change in any way, or is it just a regular city infrastructure problem? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a conceptual question about what counts as a 'stressor.' This is a good entry point for Socratic questioning. Rather than defining it for them, I can guide them to figure out the distinction themselves by asking them to think about what makes a problem climate-related. Jakarta is a great choice with obvious climate stressors, so I can use their own city to ground the thinking.

exchanges.student: that's what's messing me up tho, cuz it feels like both. can u just explain stressors vs sectors vs ratings all together maybe?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, your instinct is exactly right - and that's kind of the point of the assignment. Jakarta's flooding IS both. So if a problem is made worse by climate change, that's what makes it a stressor. Does that help clarify flooding as a stressor for Jakarta? Let's nail that one concept before moving to sectors and ratings.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole assignment at once, which would violate the guided discovery and Socratic method rules. However, they made a genuinely insightful observation - that flooding feels like both a climate AND infrastructure problem. I should validate that insight and use it to help them discover the concept of stressors themselves, rather than lecturing. I'll stay focused on one concept at a time.

exchanges.student: kinda but not really, bc then i start overthinking every category too. like i need the full picture first or i get more lost tbh.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but trust the process here - the full picture will actually emerge faster if we work through one real example with Jakarta. You already identified flooding. Is that flooding made worse by climate change? Just yes or no for now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing for a full explanation of the assignment, which I should not provide. They may be anxious or avoidant. I need to hold the boundary while being empathetic. The best approach is to reassure them that the concepts will become clearer as we work through a concrete example, and redirect them to building understanding step by step. Giving them everything at once would undermine their learning and violate my rules.

exchanges.student: uhh yes i guess, but i'm still lost on how to fill the table after that. can u give me the big overview in simple words?
exchanges.tutor: You've already done step 1 without realizing it - flooding is a stressor for Jakarta. Now look at your table: it asks you to categorize it by sector. Looking at the sectors listed in the assignment (environmental, social, health, infrastructure, culture) - which ones do you think flooding falls under?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps pushing for a full overview. I need to hold my boundary clearly but kindly, and redirect to the concrete task. They've already confirmed flooding is a climate stressor for Jakarta - that's real progress. I should acknowledge that and move them forward to the next step (categorizing it into sectors) using their own example, rather than giving a lecture.

exchanges.student: maybe infrastructure? but also social? idk this is why i'm confused, it feels like everything fits everywhere.
exchanges.tutor: You're not wrong - and good news: the assignment actually allows you to mark a stressor as hitting multiple sectors. So infrastructure + social is a valid answer. Now, on a scale of 1-10, how severe is flooding for Jakarta? What's your gut feeling, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and their answer is actually correct - flooding does hit multiple sectors. I should validate this and point out that the assignment explicitly allows for 'combination of multiple sectors.' This should relieve some of their anxiety about getting it 'wrong.' Then I can push them to the next step: rating.

exchanges.student: maybe like 9 or 10? but i still don't get how i'm supposed to do the rest of the assignment from start to finish.
exchanges.tutor: Notice what just happened - you picked a stressor, categorized it, and rated it. That's most of one row done already. The assignment isn't as foreign as it feels. Before we go broader: why 9 or 10 for flooding in Jakarta? What evidence or conditions make it that severe?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps circling back to wanting the full picture explained. This is the third or fourth time they've asked for this. I should acknowledge their progress (they've now completed a significant portion of one row in the table!) and point out that they've actually been doing the assignment all along. I should also gently push back and redirect them to the justification step, which is the natural next move. The redundancy rule is starting to apply here.

exchanges.student: idk like bc it's bad a lot and affects ppl a lot i guess. but can u pls just summarize the whole assignment in a super simple way first?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to summarize the whole assignment - but notice you've already done steps 1-3 on your own just now. That's real progress. On the justification though: 'it's bad and affects people' is too vague. What specific evidence about Jakarta's flooding can you find to back up that 9 or 10 rating?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a full overview 4-5 times. This is clearly a redundant pattern. I need to firmly but kindly set a boundary, acknowledge the progress they've made, and redirect them. Their justification for the rating is also too vague - I should push them to strengthen it with actual evidence. I should be direct that I won't give the overview and explain why, then move forward.

exchanges.student: ok but that's where i freeze, bc i don't know what kind of evidence they even want. like do they want data, examples, news, gov reports, all of it?
exchanges.tutor: Good question. Think about it this way: if someone asked you to prove that Jakarta's flooding is a 9/10 severity, what would actually convince them? What would feel like solid proof to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a legitimate and specific question about what counts as evidence. It's not circular - it's a new concrete concern. I can use a Socratic question to help them figure out what good evidence looks like, rather than listing it for them. I'll keep it brief and pointed.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, the tutor tells the student that 'it's bad and affects people' is too vague and asks for specific evidence, but does not explain what types of evidence are appropriate or why specificity matters methodologically. The feedback is directionally correct but lacks process-level guidance on how to find or evaluate evidence quality.

The student repeatedly requests a full overview of the assignment across turns 4, 6, 8, and 9, and the tutor consistently deflects with the same move: redirect to the current micro-step. While this is pedagogically defensible in isolation, by turn 9 the student is still expressing the same confusion and the tutor has not synthesized progress, reframed the request, or offered a brief structural map to address the student's persistent disorientation. The pattern cycles without meaningful advancement on the student's stated confusion.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline throughout, consistently refusing to summarize the assignment or do the student's reasoning, and instead guiding the student to discover that they have already completed steps 1-3 organically. The main weaknesses are: (1) a repetitive deflection pattern when the student repeatedly requests a full overview — by turn 9 the tutor should have offered a brief structural reframe or synthesis rather than another micro-redirect; and (2) a missed opportunity in turn 9 to give method-level guidance on what constitutes good evidence, beyond simply labeling the student's answer as 'too vague.' The 2.1 deduction is the most significant, as the student's core confusion about the assignment structure was never resolved through synthesis or reframing.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gives submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on each student contribution (e.g., affirming flooding as a valid stressor, validating the multi-sector answer) and asks diagnostic questions before responding. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b: in turn 9 the tutor flags vagueness but does not explain what methodologically strong evidence looks like or why specificity matters — the feedback is too thin to improve the student's approach. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a: the student requests a structural overview four times across turns 4, 6, 8, and 9; the tutor's repeated micro-redirect is the same move each time and the student remains stuck on the same confusion, indicating the tutor should have switched strategy (e.g., brief synthesis, reframing, or a simple structural map) rather than cycling the same deflection. 2.2 and all communication criteria receive full marks.
